questions.question:
Based only on the following passage, which one of the options can be inferred with certainty?

When the congregation sang together, Apenyo would also join, though her little screams were not quite audible because of the group singing. But whenever there was a special number, trouble would begin; Apenyo would sing along, much to the embarrassment of her mother. After two or three such mortifying Sunday evenings, the mother stopped going to church altogether until Apenyo became older and learnt to behave.

At home too, Apenyo never kept quiet; she hummed or made up silly songs to sing by herself, which annoyed her mother at times but did not particularly make her become person. She was by now convinced that her daughter had inherited her love of singing from her departed father.

(Excerpt from These Hills Called Home by Temsula Ao)

A. The mother was embarrassed about her daughter's singing at home.
B. The mother's feelings about her daughter's singing at home were of annoyance.
C. The mother was not sure if Apenyo had inherited someone else's genes.
D. When Apenyo hummed at home, her mother tended to become thoughtful.
questions.answer: D. When Apenyo hummed at home, her mother tended to become thoughtful.
questions.to_find: which inference about the mother's reaction to Apenyo's singing can be drawn with certainty

understand.plain: The passage describes Apenyo, a young girl who loves to sing and whose singing embarrasses her mother at church gatherings. At home, Apenyo's singing annoyed her mother at times, but the mother also reflected and concluded that Apenyo inherited her singing from her father. The question asks what can be inferred with certainty from the passage alone.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must be true based directly on what the passage states, with no speculation required.
keywords.example: If the passage says the mother stopped going to church, we can certainly infer she did not attend church during that period.

keywords.explain: A feeling of shame or discomfort, mentioned specifically in the context of church singing, not home singing.
keywords.example: The mother was mortified by Apenyo's public singing at church, not at home.

keywords.explain: The mother reflected on who Apenyo inherited her singing from, suggesting a contemplative mood when Apenyo sang at home.
keywords.example: She was convinced Apenyo inherited singing from her father — this shows she was thinking deeply.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a reading comprehension question about a short prose passage.

given.aim: Identify the one statement that can be certainly inferred from the passage about the mother's reaction to Apenyo's home singing.

terms.meaning: She sang along during special numbers, embarrassing her mother and causing the mother to stop attending church.
terms.example: After two or three mortifying Sunday evenings, the mother stopped going to church.
terms.connects: Establishes embarrassment is linked to public/church singing, not home singing.

terms.meaning: She hummed or made up silly songs at home, which annoyed her mother at times but did not particularly make her become a person (i.e., did not define the mother's view).
terms.example: She hummed or made up silly songs to sing by herself, which annoyed her mother at times.
terms.connects: The mother's reaction at home is mild annoyance, but also reflection.

terms.meaning: The mother became convinced that Apenyo's love of singing came from her departed father.
terms.example: She was by now convinced that her daughter had inherited her love of singing from her departed father.
terms.connects: This shows the mother became thoughtful/reflective when Apenyo sang at home, not merely annoyed or embarrassed.

given.plan: Check each option against the passage text. Option A is wrong (embarrassment is only at church). Option B is partially true but incomplete (annoyance was only 'at times'). Option C is wrong (the mother was sure about the inheritance). Option D aligns with the passage: the mother reflected on Apenyo's father when she heard singing at home, implying she became thoughtful.

steps.title: Eliminate options that contradict or go beyond the passage
steps.apply: A says mother was embarrassed about home singing — but the passage says embarrassment was only at church. B says annoyance was the mother's feeling at home — but the passage says it annoyed her 'at times' and also led to reflection. C says mother was unsure about the inheritance — but the passage says she was 'convinced'. All three can be ruled out.
steps.note: Each eliminated option either contradicts explicit passage text or adds a detail not supported.

steps.title: Verify option D against the passage
steps.apply: D says 'when Apenyo hummed at home, her mother tended to become thoughtful.' The passage states the mother was 'by now convinced' the daughter had inherited singing from her departed father. Being 'convinced' after reflection means the mother did become thoughtful when hearing the home singing. Option D is a fair and certain inference.
steps.note: The word 'thoughtful' captures the mother's reflective conviction about the father's inheritance — directly supported by the passage.

solution.result: D. When Apenyo hummed at home, her mother tended to become thoughtful.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are required.

questions.question:
Which one of the following options can be inferred from the given passage alone?

When I was a kid, I was partial to stories about other worlds and interplanetary travel. I used to imagine that I could just gaze off into space and be whisked to another planet.

(Excerpt from The Truth about Stories by Thomas King)

A. It is a child's description of what he/she liked as a child.
B. It is an adult's memory of what he or she liked as a child.
C. The child in the passage read stories about interplanetary travel only in parts.
D. It teaches us that stories are good for children.
questions.answer: B. It is an adult's memory of what he or she liked as a child.
questions.to_find: which inference about the narrator's perspective can be drawn from the passage alone

understand.plain: The narrator describes a childhood preference for stories about other worlds and interplanetary travel. The key linguistic clue is the use of past tense ('When I was a kid', 'I used to imagine'), which signals that the speaker is now an adult looking back on childhood memories. The question asks what can be inferred from the passage alone.

keywords.explain: The use of 'was a kid' and 'used to imagine' indicates the speaker is no longer a child and is recalling the past.
keywords.example: 'When I was a kid' can only be said by someone who is no longer a kid.

keywords.explain: Means having a preference or fondness for something.
keywords.example: Being partial to interplanetary stories means the child liked those stories best.

keywords.explain: The conclusion must rely only on what is written in the excerpt, not on outside knowledge or assumptions.
keywords.example: We cannot say the child read 'only in parts' (option C) — the passage says no such thing.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a verbal inference question about a short first-person passage.

given.aim: Identify the single statement about the narrator's viewpoint that is directly supported by the passage.

terms.meaning: A phrase used by someone who is now an adult, looking back at childhood.
terms.example: Only an adult can say 'when I was a kid' — this rules out option A (a child's description).
terms.connects: Signals the narrator is currently an adult, not a child.

terms.meaning: Past habitual action, indicating the imagination about space travel was something the narrator no longer does (or not in the same way).
terms.example: 'Used to' is past habitual — the narrator no longer gazes off into space in the same childlike way.

terms.term: stories about other worlds and interplanetary travel
terms.meaning: The genre of fiction the child was fond of.
terms.example: Science fiction or fantasy stories involving planets and space travel.
terms.connects: The subject matter of the childhood preference, not a statement that travel was 'only in parts'.

given.plan: Analyze the verb tenses and narrative voice. 'When I was a kid' establishes the narrator is now grown up. Option A says it is a child's description — contradicted by past tense. Option B says it is an adult's memory — directly supported. Option C says 'only in parts' — not mentioned anywhere. Option D says it teaches us stories are good — a moral not stated in the passage.

steps.title: Identify the narrator's temporal position using verb tense
steps.apply: The phrase 'When I was a kid' is past tense and is used by an adult reflecting on childhood. This immediately tells us the narrator is currently an adult. Option A (child's description) is therefore wrong — a child would say 'I am a kid' not 'I was a kid'.
steps.note: Verb tense is the critical linguistic clue in this passage.

steps.title: Verify option B and eliminate C and D
steps.apply: Option B states it is an adult's memory of what he or she liked as a child — this matches perfectly with the past-tense, retrospective narration. Option C ('only in parts') adds a detail not in the passage. Option D ('stories are good for children') is a moral lesson not stated or implied anywhere. Therefore B is the only certain inference.
steps.note: Options C and D introduce ideas external to or unsupported by the passage text.

solution.result: B. It is an adult's memory of what he or she liked as a child.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are required.

questions.question:
Which one of the options does NOT describe the passage below or follow from it?

We tend to think of cancer as a 'modern' illness because its metaphors are so modern. It is a disease of overproduction, of sudden growth, a growth that is unstoppable, tipped into the abyss of no control. Modern cell biology encourages us to imagine the cell as a machine unable to quench its initial command to grow; and thus transforms into an indestructible, self-propelled automaton.

(Adapted from The Emperor of All Maladies by Siddhartha Mukherjee)

A. It is a reflection of why cancer seems so modern to most of us.
B. It tells us that modern cell biology uses and promotes metaphors of machinery.
C. Modern cell biology describes how cancer can be cured, as the cell is often imagined as a machine.
D. Modern cell biology never uses figurative language, such as metaphors, to describe or explain anything.
questions.answer: D. Modern cell biology never uses figurative language, such as metaphors, to describe or explain anything.
questions.to_find: which option does NOT describe the passage or follow from it

understand.plain: The passage argues that cancer is perceived as a 'modern' illness because the language (metaphors) used to describe it are drawn from modern concepts — overproduction, machines, automation, and loss of control. The question is a NEGATIVE inference question: find the option that does NOT match or follow from the passage.

keywords.explain: A figure of speech that describes one thing in terms of another; here, the cell is described as a machine.
keywords.example: Calling a cancer cell an 'indestructible self-propelled automaton' is a machine metaphor.

keywords.explain: The question asks for the false or contradicted statement — the one that cannot be derived from the passage.
keywords.example: If the passage uses metaphors, any option saying biology 'never uses metaphors' directly contradicts the passage.

keywords.explain: The passage uses industrial metaphors: uncontrolled growth (factory overproduction) and self-propelled machine (automaton) to characterize cancer.
keywords.example: Cancer is compared to a machine that cannot stop running — the automaton metaphor.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a verbal reasoning question about a scientific prose passage.

given.aim: Find the one statement that is either contradicted by or cannot be derived from the passage.

terms.meaning: The passage says we call cancer 'modern' because its metaphors (overproduction, loss of control) are modern.
terms.example: Option A — 'a reflection of why cancer seems modern' — directly matches this claim.
terms.connects: Options A and B follow from the passage; D directly contradicts it.

terms.meaning: Modern cell biology uses machinery metaphors to describe the cell's behaviour in cancer.
terms.example: The passage explicitly says cell biology 'encourages us to imagine the cell as a machine'.
terms.connects: This makes option B (biology promotes metaphors of machinery) true — it follows directly.

terms.meaning: The passage itself is evidence that cell biology uses metaphors. Option D claims the opposite.
terms.example: Option D says biology 'never uses figurative language' — the passage refutes this by using machine metaphors throughout.
terms.connects: Option D directly contradicts the central evidence in the passage.

given.plan: For each option, check whether it can be derived from or is described in the passage. A is true (passage explains why cancer feels modern). B is true (passage describes cell as machine — promoting machinery metaphors). C says biology describes how to cure cancer — the passage says nothing about curing cancer, but C is only weakly related; D flatly contradicts the passage by claiming biology never uses figurative language. D is the clearest contradiction.

steps.title: Evaluate options A, B, and C against the passage
steps.apply: Option A: The passage says cancer seems modern because its metaphors are modern — A directly follows. Option B: The passage says cell biology uses the machine metaphor — B follows directly. Option C: The passage talks about imagining the cell as a machine, but says nothing about curing cancer. C is slightly off but doesn't flatly contradict the passage.
steps.note: C introduces 'cure' which is absent from the passage, but the strongest contradiction is in D.

steps.title: Evaluate option D — the direct contradiction
steps.apply: Option D states that 'modern cell biology never uses figurative language, such as metaphors'. The passage itself demonstrates that modern cell biology heavily uses figurative/metaphorical language ('cell as machine', 'indestructible self-propelled automaton', 'abyss of no control'). Option D is directly and explicitly contradicted by the passage content. This is the answer.
steps.note: D is the only option that directly contradicts what the passage explicitly states and exemplifies.

solution.result: D. Modern cell biology never uses figurative language, such as metaphors, to describe or explain anything.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are required.
